briefing.opening: Hello everyone. I will now begin the climate briefing for May 5, 2026.

sections.title: 🔴 Deepening Climate Crisis and Ecosystem Warnings
sections.content: News released today warns of the intensifying direct threats that climate change poses to our ecosystems and lives. Greenhouse gas concentrations have once again reached record highs [3], and the resulting marine heatwaves are directly impacting the catches of popular fish such as tuna and rockfish [2]. Domestically, young salmon are even becoming 'river ghosts' [1], and internationally, climate change is warming Japanese soils, leading to an increase in farmers cultivating avocados instead of citrus fruits [24, 44], while summer watermelons are also under threat [20]. With heatwave warnings issued in Bangkok, Thailand [38], heatwaves caused by climate change are emerging as serious health issues, increasing the risk of stroke and death [50]. Observations also indicate an intensification of the 'seven-day rain' phenomenon in coastal areas [47].

sections.title: ⚠️ Policy Challenges and Infrastructure Vulnerabilities
sections.content: The policy and institutional frameworks for responding to climate change continue to face numerous challenges. Specifically, repeated reports of the Trump administration's attempts to block climate change lawsuits filed by Minnesota against oil companies [16, 21, 22, 25, 28, 31, 33, 34, 35, 37, 40, 41, 42] illustrate a facet of political resistance hindering climate action. Furthermore, there have been reports of inadequate government strategies for protecting critical climate change infrastructure [43], and persistent issues include delays in flood risk map creation and the failure to incorporate climate change considerations [39, 49, 53]. These inadequate responses could lead to severe economic vulnerabilities, such as a potential reduction of up to 8.3% in Malaysia's GDP [30], threats to Tanzania's blue economy growth [46], and an anticipated 50% increase in food traceability costs due to climate change and conflict [48].

sections.title: 🌱 Accelerating Domestic and International Carbon Neutrality and Renewable Energy Transition
sections.content: Despite the challenges, domestic and international efforts for carbon neutrality and renewable energy transition continue steadily. Domestically, local-level responses are active, with Icheon City supporting farm management stability in preparation for climate change [4], and Gyeongbuk Province expanding agricultural water infrastructure in drought-prone areas [5]. ETRI is establishing an AX smart farm for carbon-neutral livestock farming in Jeju [6], and Daedeok-gu is operating a Carbon Neutrality Lifestyle Practice Center program [8], encouraging citizen participation. In the renewable energy sector, transition efforts are accelerating, with China launching renewable energy projects directly connected to data centers [9], Hongcheon-gun and Muan-gun inviting bids for integrated renewable energy support projects [10, 12], and EWP (East-West Power) discussing strengthening renewable energy safety nets with its wind power affiliates [11]. Last week, a climate conference was held in Colombia to end the fossil fuel era, accelerating the renewable energy transition [54], and nations have put the 'net-zero' agreement back on track to curb global shipping emissions [55]. The International Energy Agency (IEA) emphasized that addressing methane emissions is crucial for both climate change and energy security [15].

sections.title: 🌍 Climate Change Adaptation and Shifting Social Perceptions
sections.content: Social awareness of climate change is gradually expanding, and adaptation and response efforts are being made in various ways. While concerns about climate change are surging in Asia, public support for related policies has been reported to vary [45]. New York officials are combating climate change by promoting composting [19], and coffee grower cooperatives are showing new initiatives due to climate change [32]. Examples include Victoria's Circus combining art and marine science to address climate change [36], and research suggesting that the mysterious Amazonian 'dark earth' could help in climate change response [29], both demonstrating creative approaches. Meanwhile, instances such as snakes in Georgia adapting to climate change [23] and Ulukhaktok residents recounting firsthand observations of land changes [52] vividly illustrate how ecosystems and local communities are responding to climate change. Even unusual cases, such as king penguins benefiting from climate change, have been observed [51].

briefing.closing: Today's briefing has highlighted both the severity of the climate crisis and the diverse efforts and challenges involved in overcoming it. Only sustained attention and action can safeguard our future. This concludes today's climate briefing.

articles.original_title: ‘Historic breakthrough’: could the fossil fuel era be coming to an end? – podcast
articles.description: The transition towards renewable energy received a boost last week when representatives from 57 countries met in Santa Marta, Colombia, for a world-first climate meeting aimed at bringing the fossil fuels era to an end. Madeleine Finlay hears from the Guardian’s global environment editor, Jon Watts, about how the landmark conference came about, who was missing, and whether the optimism can translate into real world actionCould Santa Marta climate talks mark ground zero in push to ditch fossil fu

summary.phenomenon: Last week, representatives from 57 countries gathered in Santa Marta, Colombia, for a world-first climate meeting aimed at ending the fossil fuel era, thereby accelerating the transition to renewable energy.
summary.outlook: It remains to be seen whether the optimism from this landmark conference can translate into concrete actions signaling the end of the fossil fuel era.
